content:
**Constraints:**
- Use only two sub-structures.

**Goal:**
- Build a single rocket engine capable of providing propulsion to a single direction.
- Build a simple chassis to assemble the rocket engines using braces to form a symmetric rocket.
- The assembled rocket should be able to lift off from the ground to the sky in the simulation environment.

**Evaluation Protocol:**
- The assembled rocket will be placed at position (x=0, y=0, z=0) on the ground plane.
- During the simulation, the firing control key of the rocket engine will be pressed and held continuously.
- The motion trajectory of the assembled rocket will be recorded throughout the simulation.

**Scoring Metrics:**
- *Maximum Height:* The highest vertical position (z) reached by any block of the assembled rocket (higher is better).
- *Trajectory Deviation:* The average lateral distance between the assembled rocket’s actual trajectory and the ideal vertical line (smaller is better).
- *Maximum Speed:* The highest speed achieved by any block of the assembled rocket (higher is better).
- *Cost:* The total number of blocks used to construct the assembled rocket (fewer is better).

<building_plan>
  <overall_structure>
    <description>
      The rocket is a rigid, symmetric assembly made from two identical steam rocket engine modules mounted on opposite sides of a light central chassis spine using solid braces. Each engine module is a downward-pointing Water Cannon that is locally heated by a Torch to run in steam mode (8.6× thrust), mounted under a small wooden mount block. Two small wooden “lug” blocks on the north and south sides of each engine provide brace anchor faces. The central chassis is a simple two-block vertical stack above its own base stone that offers east and west faces at two heights for bracing. Four Braces (two per engine, at two different heights) tie the engines to the chassis to hold the engines vertical and centered, producing a symmetric, vertical-thrust rocket.
    </description>
    <functionality>
      - Propulsion: Each Water Cannon points straight down and, when heated by its Torch, produces steam-mode thrust. Two engines fire simultaneously to produce a strong, symmetric upward force.
      - Stability: Two braces per engine (upper and lower) form rigid triangles to the chassis, resisting pitch/roll and keeping thrust vectors aligned with +z (up).
      - Lift-off feasibility: Each engine provides 1.6 units (water) × 8.6 ≈ 13.76 mass-equivalent thrust in steam mode. Two engines yield ≈27.52. Estimated assembled mass ≈10.15 (see breakdown in sub-structures and connectors), producing initial net upward acceleration >1 g, ensuring lift-off and high maximum height/speed with low lateral deviation due to symmetry.
    </functionality>
    <assembly>
      - Instantiate two copies of Sub-Structure 1 (Steam Rocket Engine Module): Engine-L (west side) and Engine-R (east side).
      - Build one copy of Sub-Structure 2 (Central Chassis Spine) at the origin.
      - Spatial arrangement (plan view): Place Engine-L to the west (negative x) of the chassis and Engine-R to the east (positive x), at the same z level so their mount blocks are aligned with the chassis stack. Keep several block-widths of lateral clearance so torches and cannons do not intersect the chassis; braces will span the gap.
      - Bracing (4 total):
        • Engine-L: connect a Brace from the outward face of its north lug to the west face of the upper chassis block; connect a second Brace from the outward face of its south lug to the west face of the lower chassis block.
        • Engine-R: connect a Brace from the outward face of its north lug to the east face of the upper chassis block; connect a second Brace from the outward face of its south lug to the east face of the lower chassis block.
      - Ensure all braces are taut and of similar length left/right to preserve symmetry. Maintain enough lateral spacing so the torches sit clear of the chassis and each other, and so cannon outlets are a small distance above ground at rest to avoid clipping.
    </assembly>
    <motion_control>
      - Configure both Water Cannons (Engine-L and Engine-R) to the same firing control key (e.g., “F”). The evaluation will hold this key continuously.
      - Torches are passive and remain lit; no control needed.
      - Expected motion: Upon firing, torches heat the cannon bodies into steam mode within the small spherical heating area (radius 0.3) adjacent to the lower side of each cannon. Thrust vectors are vertical and balanced, so the rocket rises approximately straight up with minimal lateral deviation. Two-height bracing suppresses pitch/yaw/roll.
    </motion_control>
  </overall_structure>

  <sub_structures>
    <sub_structure_1>
      <name>Steam Rocket Engine Module</name>
      <description>
        A compact, self-contained steam rocket engine that generates vertical thrust. It consists of a small wooden mount block attached to its base stone, a Water Cannon mounted to the mount’s bottom face pointing downward, a Torch on the mount’s side pointing downward to heat the cannon into steam mode, and two opposite small wooden lug blocks (north and south) used solely as brace anchor points to connect to the chassis.
      </description>
      <components>
        - 1x Default Stone Base (1×1×1, mass 0.25)
        - 1x Small Wooden Block (mount, mass 0.3)
        - 1x Water Cannon (mass 1.5)
        - 1x Torch (mass 1.0)
        - 2x Small Wooden Block (brace lugs: north and south, each mass 0.3)
      </components>
      <assembly>
        - Start from the sub-structure’s default stone base.
        - Attach the mount Small Wooden Block (Wm) to the TOP face of the base stone (centers aligned).
        - Attach the Water Cannon to the BOTTOM face of Wm, outlet pointing straight DOWN (−z). This orients thrust upward (+z).
        - Attach a Torch to the EAST face of Wm, oriented DOWNWARD. The torch’s horizontal support offsets ~0.5 out from Wm’s east face; its heating sphere (radius 0.3) is centered ~1.0 below the torch body. This places the heating sphere adjacent to the lower side of the Water Cannon body (clear of the direct exhaust axis), reliably heating it to steam mode.
        - Attach two Small Wooden Blocks as brace lugs: one to the NORTH face of Wm (lug-N) and one to the SOUTH face of Wm (lug-S). Their outward faces (north of lug-N and south of lug-S) will be kept free for Braces during final assembly.
        - Face use tally (to avoid conflicts): Wm top used by base; bottom used by cannon; east used by torch; north and south used by lugs; west remains free.
        - Clearance: Ensure the Water Cannon hangs below Wm with its outlet below Wm but slightly above the ground upon final assembly; the Torch sits lateral to the cannon, not directly under the outlet.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - Assign the Water Cannon to the shared firing key (e.g., “F”).
        - Torch is always on (no control).
        - Behavior: Holding the key runs the cannon; torch heating transitions output to steam mode (≈8.6× thrust), producing strong upward thrust.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        Provides one vertical steam rocket engine with integral heating and brace anchors. Two instances are used to create a symmetric propulsion pair for the final rocket.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - The Water Cannon must point straight down with its axis aligned to global z.
        - The Torch’s heating sphere (radius 0.3) must intersect the Water Cannon’s lower side body (avoid the narrow waist); place the torch slightly lateral and below the mount so it is not directly in the exhaust stream to reduce extinguishing risk.
        - Keep lug-N and lug-S outward faces free for Braces.
        - Ensure ground clearance for the cannon outlet at rest; adjust engine-to-chassis spacing via brace lengths as needed.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_1>

    <sub_structure_2>
      <name>Central Chassis Spine</name>
      <description>
        A minimal, lightweight vertical spine that provides two bracing faces per side (east and west) at two different heights. It serves as the central mass and rigid anchor for the two engine modules.
      </description>
      <components>
        - 1x Default Stone Base (1×1×1, mass 0.25)
        - 2x Small Wooden Block (stacked vertically above the base, each mass 0.3)
      </components>
      <assembly>
        - Attach the first Small Wooden Block (C0) to the TOP face of the chassis base stone.
        - Attach the second Small Wooden Block (C1) to the TOP face of C0 (stacked).
        - Keep the EAST and WEST faces of both C0 and C1 free for Braces. This yields two bracing heights per side.
        - The NORTH/SOUTH faces remain open for future extensions if needed but are unused in this plan.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - None. The chassis is passive.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        Provides symmetric, rigid mounting points to tie in the two engine modules with triangular bracing, keeping thrust vectors vertical and aligned through the rocket’s centerline for stable ascent.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - Maintain both EAST and WEST faces of C0 (lower) and C1 (upper) unoccupied to accept Braces from engines.
        - Position the chassis between the two engines with roughly equal lateral spacing left/right; brace lengths should be similar for symmetry.
        - Ensure at-rest ground clearance so engine outlets are just above the ground plane after assembly.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_2>
  </sub_structures>
</building_plan>
